text: The surgeon inserts a 5mm optical trocar into an incision in the left upper quadrant, navigating behind the thin rectus muscle. The trocar is advanced slowly, with careful adjustments to ensure proper orientation. The surgeon observes the tissue color, noting red areas and searching for white tissue to confirm the correct positioning of the scope.

The surgeon carefully navigates through the fat layer, allowing CO₂ insufflation to expand the surgical field. The laparoscope is directed caudally, but visual cues suggest a medial and cephalad orientation. The obturator is removed for better visualization, revealing the white posterior rectus sheath, confirming correct placement in the surgical space.

The surgeon carefully lifts the fibroareolar tissue against the posterior rectus sheath, avoiding damage to nerves and vessels in the retroactive space. The space has developed well, and the surgeon uses a spinal needle to locate the position for the second port, which is close to the end of the existing five-millimeter port.

The surgeon carefully navigates around a large neurovascular bundle, moving the camera port more cephalad and laterally. XPREL solution is infiltrated for a tap block and rectus sheath block. The surgeon slowly dilates the port through the rectus muscle, ensuring the tip remains visible to prevent space collapse. If the posterior rectus sheath and peritoneum are punctured, the surgeon uses a Maryland instrument to stent the space open.

The surgeon meticulously ensures hemostasis while creating space for the third port insertion in the ETEP procedure. The surgical field is kept blood-free to maintain clear visibility of anatomical structures. The surgeon identifies a window of bare muscle, avoiding the inferior epigastric vessels, to place the third port approximately seven to eight centimeters apart from the others.

The surgeon inserts a spinal needle alongside the port to ensure the operator tip follows the intended path without collapsing the space. The camera is moved to the lower port to view the needle entering lateral to the port into the areolar tissue. The surgeon performs a rectus sheath block on the contralateral side using ultrasound, injecting 10 mLs of XPREL solution into the areolar tissue to target neurovascular bundles.

The surgeon adjusts the patient's position by flexing the bed to lower the legs, ensuring that the robot instruments will not collide with them during the procedure. The camera port is inspected to confirm that surrounding tissue will not obstruct the view, and the area appears clear, allowing for the docking of the robotic system.
